nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef walls, ledges and rubble slopes with moderate current; tends to anchor among rockwork where suspended particulates and plankton drift past.

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with stable ledges and crevices to anchor in
Areas of moderate flow where suspended particulates drift past
Open water column above the perch for tentacle extension
Pump intakes and overflows fully screened to prevent injury or whole-tank toxin release

diet.notes: Obligate suspension/filter feeder. Extends a crown of feathery yellow-and-pink tentacles to catch phytoplankton, zooplankton and fine organic particulates from the water column; particles are then transferred one tentacle at a time to the mouth. Has been reported to do best in mature reef tanks with naturally elevated plankton loads, supplemented daily with targeted dosing of liquid invertebrate foods, phytoplankton, or zooplankton suspensions. Often refuses or cannot use particulate foods that settle quickly.

summary: Small filter-feeding sea cucumber with showy pink-and-yellow feeding tentacles. Generally considered reef-safe and popular in nano reefs, but is an obligate plankton feeder and carries the usual cucumber toxin-release risk if stressed.
careNotes: The Strawberry Sea Cucumber is a small dendrochirote cucumber (around 5–12 cm at maturity, depending on source) that typically anchors itself on a piece of rock in moderate flow and extends a crown of bright pink-and-yellow feathery tentacles to filter feed. Unlike the larger and more notorious Sea Apple (Pseudocolochirus axiologus), it is generally regarded as a lower-risk choice for established nano and small reef tanks, but the same fundamental husbandry concerns apply: it is an obligate suspension feeder, and like all sea cucumbers it can release holothurin-type toxins capable of killing tankmates if it dies or is shredded by a pump. All pump intakes, powerheads and overflow boxes should be fully screened before introduction, and any specimen that is clearly failing should be removed promptly. Drip acclimation over at least 1–2 hours is generally advised; the species is intolerant of copper-based medications and sensitive to swings in salinity, pH, oxygen and nitrate. A mature tank (commonly recommended at 6+ months) with naturally elevated plankton helps, alongside daily targeted feeding of phytoplankton and liquid invertebrate suspensions. Sources disagree on adult size — LiveAquaria reports up to roughly 5 in (~12 cm) while several aquarium summaries describe ~5 cm specimens — so a 5–12 cm working range is used; this is also why a 120 L tank is recommended over the LiveAquaria-quoted 30 gal floor, biased slightly upward toward mature-reef stability. Note that this species is sometimes confused or co-traded with Colochirus quadrangularis (Thorny Sea Cucumber); they are biologically distinct but have very similar husbandry.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care reference (Spiny Sea Cucumber, Green w/Pink & Yellow / Pentacta anceps). Listed as Expert Only, reef-compatible, peaceful, max ~5 in, 30 gal minimum, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025. Daily feeding with zooplankton or liquid invertebrate food; toxin-risk on death; copper-intolerant.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Assigned secondary source did not surface a species-specific page for Pentacta anceps on direct search; swapped to alternative secondaries below per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Tank Facts — Green w/Pink & Yellow Spiny Sea Cucumber (Pentacta anceps)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (76–80°F, pH 7.8–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.026, NO3 1–20 ppm) and the >30 gal minimum tank size. Sourced filter-feeding behaviour description and stationary-perch placement. Tank Facts lists family as Holothuriidae, which is incorrect for this genus; Cucumariidae was used instead, confirmed via Wikipedia/GBIF.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pentacta (genus)
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy: family Cucumariidae, order Dendrochirotida, class Holothuroidea.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Colochirus quadrangularis
additional.notes: Reference for the often-confused Thorny Sea Cucumber (Colochirus quadrangularis); used to note the trade-name overlap with this entry and to cross-check Indo-Pacific distribution, ~10 cm size, and SG 1.022–1.025 range expected of similar dendrochirotes.

additional.name: SeaLifeBase — Pentacta anceps
additional.notes: Used for native-range (Indo-West Pacific including Vietnam), benthic habitat, and depth range 0–50 m. Direct summary page returned 403; relied on indexed metadata.
